title: AML Agents Briefing — Wednesday, 06 May 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing for Wednesday the sixth of May 2026. This is your audio overview of the most material AML and financial crime developments across Asia-Pacific today. Full detail, the obligation register, source links, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Big day, Alex. We've got the FSB's finalised insurer resolution guidance, a forty-seven million Australian dollar enforcement action against a digital currency exchange, and fresh AUSTRAC guidance landing for legal practitioners under Tranche 2. Let's get into it.

ALEX: Starting with the Financial Stability Board. On the twenty-ninth of April, the FSB published its finalised guidance on which insurers fall within the scope of recovery and resolution planning requirements under the Key Attributes of Effective Resolution Regimes, the 2011 framework that underpins resolution planning globally. This closes out a consultation the FSB ran in November 2025 and narrows a long-standing ambiguity about which insurers must maintain credible recovery and resolution plans. The FSB also revised its practices paper on identifying critical functions of insurers, adding clarifying language on how the FSB's own definition of critical function maps onto national resolution frameworks.

JORDAN: So why does this matter for compliance leaders in APAC specifically?

ALEX: Because large, internationally active insurers operating across APAC jurisdictions now have clearer criteria for whether they are in scope. Group-level compliance officers at insurers with cross-border footprints in markets like Australia, Singapore, Hong Kong, and Japan need to check whether their entity satisfies the FSB's revised identification criteria. The consultation response summary the FSB published alongside the final report signals that some jurisdictions pushed back on the breadth of the original proposals, so the final scope is narrower than the November 2025 draft.

JORDAN: Right, and the read-across for heads of financial crime compliance at large insurers is fairly direct. Take the FSB's finalised criteria and map them against your group structure this week. If you are in scope, your resolution planning workstream now has a clearer regulatory baseline. Brief your board risk committee before the next scheduled meeting, and make sure your group head of financial crime is across the interaction between resolution planning obligations and your existing AML programme governance. These are not separate tracks.

ALEX: Moving on, and this one is squarely operational. AUSTRAC has announced a forty-seven million Australian dollar civil penalty against a major AUSTRAC-registered digital currency exchange for systemic failures across three areas: transaction monitoring, mule account detection, and suspicious matter report timeliness. This caps a twelve-month investigation. The size of the penalty sits in the same order of magnitude as earlier landmark AUSTRAC actions, and the specificity of the three failure categories is a clear signal of supervisory priorities for the DCE sector.

JORDAN: What stands out to me is the mule account detection piece. That is not a novel failure, but regulators are clearly still finding it.

ALEX: Exactly. AUSTRAC's focus on post-onboarding velocity spikes and inbound flows from multiple unrelated retail senders echoes the typology language in Bank Negara Malaysia's Q1 2026 bulletin on investment scams routed through digital banks and crypto exchanges. The pattern is consistent across jurisdictions: rapid account activity after a low-friction onboarding, followed by immediate outbound movement.

JORDAN: So for AML compliance officers at digital currency exchanges and virtual asset service providers across the region, here is the action. Pull your last three months of transaction monitoring alert data and check your coverage for post-onboarding velocity. If your rules don't have a dedicated scenario for inbound from multiple unrelated retail senders followed by rapid outbound, add it to your next tuning cycle. And if you are sitting on overdue suspicious matter reports, clear that backlog before your next supervisory touchpoint. The AUSTRAC enforcement makes clear that timeliness is not a secondary concern.

JORDAN: Shifting focus, I want to pick up the AUSTRAC Tranche 2 guidance for legal practitioners that landed on the twenty-sixth of April. AUSTRAC has published detailed industry guidance covering customer engagement triggers, source-of-wealth diligence in conveyancing transactions, legal professional privilege carve-outs, and the practicalities of suspicious matter report submission for law firms. This is Phase 2 of the Tranche 2 rollout, and it is the most operationally specific guidance the sector has seen.

ALEX: And the privilege carve-out question has been the live issue for legal practitioners since Tranche 2 was first announced. The guidance appears to draw a clear line between advice that attracts privilege and the designated services that do not.

JORDAN: For compliance officers at law firms or at the professional service firms advising them, the action is to read the AUSTRAC guidance against your existing onboarding procedures for conveyancing and corporate transactions. If you do not yet have a customer due diligence trigger map for the designated services covered by Tranche 2, build one now. The guidance gives you the scaffolding. And if you are advising law firm clients, update your readiness gap assessment to reflect the Phase 2 guidance before the end of this month.

ALEX: Before we close, two obligations worth flagging. Indonesia's annual AML and CFT programme review under POJK 12 is due to the Risk Oversight Committee and the Board of Commissioners by the thirtieth of June. And the HKMA self-assessment return for the 2024 reporting period is due by the thirtieth of September using the prescribed Excel template with internal audit validation.

JORDAN: So for the coming week, three concrete steps. First, if you are at an internationally active insurer, map your group structure against the FSB's finalised scope criteria. Second, if you are at a digital currency exchange or virtual asset service provider anywhere in APAC, review your mule detection scenario coverage today. Third, if you have a Tranche 2 legal practitioner readiness workstream, schedule a session this week to work the new AUSTRAC guidance into your programme.

JORDAN: Full detail on all of today's stories, the underlying regulatory sources, the obligation register, and the horizon-scanning feed are at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

ALEX: That's the briefing for Wednesday the sixth of May. We'll be back tomorrow. Stay sharp.
